children.title: 1.1 Overview
children.description: This section provides an architectural overview of the NVM Express® specification ecosystem, defining the hierarchy and relationships between individual technical specifications. It addresses fundamental inquiries regarding the scope, structure, and documentation standards of NVMe™ technology.

children.title: 1.1.1 NVM Express® Specification Family
children.description: This section outlines the NVMe® specification family architecture, detailing how the Base Specification integrates with various I/O Command Sets and Transport protocols like PCIe®, RDMA, and Fibre Channel. It defines the hierarchical relationship between these standards and directs users to official resources for revision change logs and compliance requirements.

children.title: 1.4 Conventions
children.description: This section defines the document’s technical syntax, covering standard keywords, quantitative formatting, and data-width relationships between bytes, words, and dwords. It provides the necessary framework for interpreting architecture-specific numerical descriptions and memory-addressing conventions used throughout the documentation.

children.title: 1.4.1 Keywords
children.description: This section defines the semantic standardized terminology used throughout the specification, covering technical imperatives like "shall," "should," "mandatory," and "reserved." It establishes the normative and informative constraints governing requirements, compliance, and language usage essential for interpreting system specifications.

children.title: 1.4.1.3 obsolete
children.description: This section defines the scope and limitations of the NVMe Base Specification 2.3, categorizing controller types (I/O, Discovery, and Administrative) and clarifying that specific NVM usage models, such as cache or main memory implementations, fall outside the specification's mandate. It outlines communication interface requirements for NVM subsystems while explicitly noting that physical hardware implementation remains vendor-agnostic.

children.title: 1.4.1.8 should
children.description: This section defines key terminology—including **optional**, **reserved**, **shall**, and **should**—to establish mandatory requirements and implementation guidelines for NVM Express® specification compliance. It provides precise definitions for interpretation of keywords that dictate hardware design obligations and the handling of reserved bit-fields.

children.title: 1.4.2 Numerical Descriptions
children.description: Section 1.4.2 defines the technical terminology and normative language used throughout the NVM Express® specification, specifically covering keywords such as "optional," "reserved," "shall," and "should." It establishes mandatory compliance requirements for reserved fields, bitwise handling, and implementation choices to ensure cross-vendor interoperability.

children.title: 1.4.3 Byte, Word, and Dword Relationships
children.description: This section defines the structural relationship between bytes, words, dwords, and qwords within the NVMe specification. It clarifies data addressing, bit-length hierarchies, and the standard use of little-endian formatting for technical interpretation and data storage.

children.title: 1.5 Definitions
children.description: Section 1.5 serves as the formal glossary for NVMe-over-Fabrics terminology, defining essential technical concepts such as Administrative controllers, Association, Capsules, and various Discovery controller types (CDC/DDC). It provides precise, standardized definitions for architectural components and operational mechanisms to ensure consistency in system design and troubleshooting implementation.

children.title: 1.5.2 admin label ASCII
children.description: Byte/Word/Dword layout, little‑endian ordering, byte offsets, word boundaries, dword composition, qword omission, NVM‑Express admin label ASCII mapping; answers how bytes group into words/dwords, how offsets are numbered, and what endian format the specification assumes.

children.title: 1.5.14 candidate command
children.description: This section defines foundational NVMe management terminology, specifically detailing the Admin Queue's structure, the Administrative controller's functional limitations, and technical definitions for arbitration mechanisms and namespace allocation. It serves as a reference for identifying key controller roles, command execution flow, and structural constraints within an NVM subsystem.

children.title: 1.5.23 Directive
children.description: Section 1.5.23 defines fundamental NVMe terminology, including **capsule**, **controller** types, **command submission**, and **Configurable Device Personality (CDP)**. This section provides a technical glossary that establishes standard definitions for NVMe over Fabrics and memory-based transport models to resolve questions regarding NVM subsystem communication and configuration.

children.title: 1.5.36 Exported NVM Resources
children.description: This section defines key NVMe architecture terminology, including Discovery controllers, Discovery Services, dispersed namespaces, and various controller types such as dynamic, embedded management, and emulated controllers. It serves as a reference to classify foundational NVM subsystem components and explain their specific roles within an NVMe fabric or storage environment.

children.title: 1.5.42 firmware/boot partition image update command sequence
children.description: This section details the firmware update command sequence, covering partition image flashing, bootloader configuration, and system recovery protocols. It answers how to safely update device firmware and execute partition-specific write operations within a boot environment.

children.title: 1.5.47 host memory
children.description: Defines exported NVM subsystem components—Exported Namespaces, Controllers, Exported Ports, Allowed Host List—and details Exported Port ID usage for fabric transport, Fabric Zoning access control, and host‑accessible memory. Explains firmware/boot‑partition image update command sequence, firmware‑slot allocation, host role, and host interaction with Submission Queues and Completion Queues.

children.title: 1.5.59 MMC
children.description: Section 1.5.59 defines core NVMe technical terminology, including **idempotent commands**, **Identify** data structures (Controller and Namespace), and **I/O queue** architecture. It establishes precise definitions for controller types and command handling to clarify operational state behaviors and namespace identification mechanisms within the NVM subsystem.

children.title: 1.5.72 NVMe Transport
children.description: This section defines critical NVMe architectural terminology ranging from migration management entities (MMH, MMHD, MMHS) to foundational components such as namespaces, NSIDs, and NVM subsystems. It provides authoritative definitions for technical concepts essential to understanding host-to-controller communication, fabric interface protocols, and lifecycle management within the NVMe 2.3 specification.

children.title: 1.5.76 physical fabric interface (physical ports)
children.description: This section details the physical fabric interface, covering high-speed port configurations, transceiver specifications, and link-layer connectivity requirements. It addresses questions regarding hardware compatibility, port density, physical media types, and cabling standards necessary for establishing robust fabric-level communications.

children.title: 1.5.84 primary controller
children.description: Defines primary controller (1.5.84) and NVMe transport binding terminology—participating NVM subsystem, personality settings, physical fabric interface, Physical Presence Indicator (PPI), Placement Handle/Identifier, Port ID, and Ports List—explaining their definitions, relationships, and how they constrain reliable delivery of data, commands, and responses across NVMe transports.

children.title: 1.5.97 spindown
children.description: This section defines essential NVMe terminology including private namespaces, properties, Reclaim Groups (RG), Reclaim Units (RU), and Reclaim Unit Handles (RUH). It provides technical definitions for rotational media, Runtime D3 (RTD3) power states, and sanitize operations to clarify controller resource management and data security protocols within the NVM Express specification.

children.title: 1.5.104 user data
children.description: This section provides technical definitions for NVMe terminology, covering concepts such as spinup, static controllers, and underlying namespace structures. It clarifies the data composition of user data while establishing foundational vocabulary for NVM subsystem management and I/O command set specifications.

children.title: 1.6 I/O Command Set specific definitions used in this specification
children.description: This section defines specialized I/O command parameters and data metrics, including Endurance Group, SMART telemetry, and User Data format configurations. It clarifies technical specifications for command execution, reporting structures, and data formatting requirements within the I/O command set.

children.title: 1.6.5 User Data Format
children.description: This section defines terminology for NVM Express architectural components, specifically covering **spindles**, **static controllers**, and **underlying namespace** infrastructures. It establishes standard definitions for **user data**—including host-stored information and associated metadata—to clarify foundational concepts for NVM subsystem configuration and I/O command set specifications.

children.title: 1.6.6 User Data Out Command
children.description: This section defines the "User Data Out Command" as an I/O command where the controller writes data that may not require a host-to-controller transfer, while also establishing essential NVM Command Set terminology like "logical block" and "LBA." It clarifies foundational definitions and provides necessary bibliographic references to industry standards concerning data sanitization, security algorithms, and transport protocols.

children.title: 1.7 NVM Command Set specific definitions used in this specification
children.description: This section defines fundamental NVMe storage concepts, specifically **logical blocks** and **logical block addresses (LBA)**. It provides the technical foundation for data addressing and unit-based storage management within the NVM Command Set.

children.title: 1.7.2 logical block address (LBA)
children.description: This section defines the fundamental terminology for NVMe storage addressing, specifically identifying the **logical block** as the minimal addressable unit and the **Logical Block Address (LBA)** as its corresponding reference index. It provides the technical basis for how host systems locate and access user data segments within the NVM Command Set.

children.title: 1.8 References
children.description: This section provides a formal bibliography of external technical standards, including CNSA cryptography, IEEE 2883 sanitization, SCSI, and ISO data interchange specifications. It serves as the primary reference list for defining protocol-specific terminology and regulatory compliance mandates required for NVM Express implementation.

children.title: 1.9 References Under Development
children.description: This section enumerates the reference standards cited for the specification—NVM Express Base Specification 2.3, TCG Storage Opal SSC Feature Set PSID 1.00, TCG Opal Specification 2.02, NIST SP 800‑57 Part 1 Rev 5, and NIST FIPS 180‑4—answering which cryptographic, storage‑security, and key‑management documents underpin the technical requirements.

children.title: 2 Theory of Operation
children.description: This section outlines the architectural foundation of the NVM Express 2.3 interface, focusing on high-performance command submission, scalable I/O queue parallelism, and enterprise-grade features like virtualization and data protection. It answers how NVMe optimizes command paths and manages system resources, utilizing specific technical mechanisms such as MMIO reduction, MSI-X interrupt aggregation, and T10 DIF/DIX standards.

children.title: 2.1 Memory-Based Transport Model (PCIe)
children.description: This section outlines the NVMe command set architecture, covering Admin, I/O, and Fabrics command categories within the PCIe transport model. It defines the specific functional roles and submission queue requirements for NVM, Zoned Namespace, Key Value, and Fabrics command types.

children.title: 2.2 Message-Based Transport Model (Fabrics)
children.description: Section 2.2 defines the NVMe-over-Fabrics (NVMe-oF) message-based architecture, focusing on discovery services, NVM subsystem ports, and capsule-based data transfer mechanisms. It addresses how fabric-agnostic transports facilitate host-to-controller communication, authentication protocols, and the establishment of scalable, remote NVMe connectivity.

children.title: 2.2.1 Fabrics and Transports
children.description: This section details the architectural distinctions between NVMe message-based Fabrics and memory-based transport models, specifically addressing I/O queue mappings, host interrupt generation, and administrative command constraints. It answers how NVMe over Fabrics mandates the exclusive use of SGLs, forbids metadata pointer usage, and defines unique procedures for queue management and host interface integration.

children.title: 2.2.2 NVM Subsystem Ports for Fabrics
children.description: This section defines NVM subsystem ports as the primary protocol interfaces between an NVMe subsystem and a fabric, detailing their role in connecting hosts to controllers via 16-bit Port IDs and NVM Subsystem NQNs. It addresses how physical fabric interfaces are aggregated into logical ports and how multiple NVMe transports are supported within the fabric-based NVMe architecture.

children.title: 2.2.4 Capsules and Data Transfer
children.description: This section outlines NVMe over Fabrics (NVMe-oF) controller-host associations and the Discovery Service mechanism. It addresses how hosts locate accessible NVM subsystems, identify multi-path connections, and establish sessions via Discovery controllers.

children.title: 2.2.5 Authentication
children.description: This section details NVMe‑over‑Fabrics authentication mechanisms—including fabric secure channel, NVMe in‑band authentication, SGL handling in command/response capsules, Discovery Service indications, and Connect response requirements—answering how a subsystem enforces secure channels, when authentication is mandatory, and how data placement and transfer restrictions are defined per transport binding. It also explains controller behavior for rejecting commands without required authentication and the role of SGLs in message‑only and message/memory transport models.

children.title: 2.3 NVM Storage Model
children.description: This section details the NVM storage model, covering hierarchy, storage entities, and I/O command sets. It addresses how NVM subsystems are architected and provides technical insights into organized data management and protocol interaction.

children.title: 2.3.1 Storage Entities
children.description: This section defines the NVMe over Fabrics response capsule structure, SGL requirements for command and data transport, and mandatory security protocols. It addresses technical specifications for capsule data placement, fabric secure channels, and in-band authentication requirements.

children.title: 2.3.3 NVM Subsystem Examples
children.description: This section defines the hierarchical architecture of an NVM subsystem, detailing the relationships between controllers, domains, endurance groups, NVM sets, and namespaces. It addresses how I/O command sets—such as NVM or Key Value—are assigned to namespaces and explains the fundamental physical-to-logical organization of storage resources within an SSD.

children.title: 2.4 Extended Capabilities Theory
children.description: This section explores advanced NVMe architecture through Multi-Path I/O, namespace sharing, and Asymmetric Controller Behavior (ACB). It addresses how enterprise storage systems optimize path redundancy, manage concurrent controller access, and handle varying performance states to ensure high availability and load balancing.

children.title: 2.4.1 Multi-Path I/O and Namespace Sharing
children.description: This section defines Multi-Path I/O and namespace sharing within the NVMe architecture, detailing the requirements for NVM subsystem configurations involving multiple controllers. It addresses how complex subsystems enable concurrent host access and independent path redundancy, clarifying the operational relationship between controllers, namespaces, and NSIDs.

children.title: 2.4.2 Asymmetric Controller Behavior
children.description: Section 2.4.2 defines asymmetric controller behavior in NVM subsystems, addressing how performance and namespace access characteristics vary based on internal configurations or specific controller paths. It introduces Asymmetric Namespace Access Reporting as a mechanism for hosts to manage these variations across single or multi-port NVM Express architectures, including those utilizing SR-IOV.

children.title: 3.1 NVM Controller Architecture
children.description: This section details NVM controller architectures, covering PCIe memory-based models, NVMe-over-Fabrics (NVMe-oF) message-based communication, and specific controller types and properties. It addresses fundamental questions regarding how controllers interface with host systems, manage command processing, and define operational characteristics within NVMe storage subsystems.

children.title: 3.1.1 Memory-Based Controller Architecture (PCIe)
children.description: This section details the hardware architecture of PCIe-based memory controllers, focusing on transaction layer packet (TLP) processing, DMA engine integration, and memory mapping mechanisms. It addresses how the controller manages high-speed data transfers between host system memory and peripheral devices while maintaining bus protocol consistency.

children.title: 3.1.2 Message-Based Controller Architecture (Fabrics)
children.description: This section defines the static and dynamic controller models for NVMe Message-Based Controllers (Fabrics), detailing how controller state persistence functions across associations and resets. It clarifies the architectural requirements for these models and specifies the distinct constraints applicable to Discovery controllers versus other message-based controller types.

children.title: 3.1.3 Controller Types
children.description: Controller association termination conditions (shutdown, Controller Level Reset, Admin Queue transport

children.title: 3.1.3.1 I/O Controller
children.description: This section defines NVMe command processing, covering asynchronous command execution, persistent data commit ordering, and Submission Queue (SQ) arbitration-based priority scheduling. It addresses requirements for host-enforced serialization, Completion Queue (CQ) notification mechanisms, and how hosts correlate command IDs with asynchronous completion entries.

children.title: 3.1.3.2 Administrative Controller
children.description: This section defines the **Administrative Controller** as a specialized NVMe component, detailing its role in providing **NVM subsystem management capabilities**—such as health status polling, enclosure management, and virtualization—without supporting I/O command processing. It addresses how these controllers differ from I/O controllers, their reliance on **NVMe-MI commands**, and their capacity for dedicated management driver integration.

children.title: 3.1.3.3 Discovery Controller
children.description: This section defines the **Discovery Controller** within the **NVMe over Fabrics (NVMe-oF)** architecture, detailing its specific role in enabling hosts to locate and connect to additional **NVM subsystems** or discovery subsystems. It serves as a foundational reference for understanding network-based device enumeration and administrative controller functionality within the NVMe 2.3 specification framework.

children.title: 3.1.3.3.1 Discovery Controller Asynchronous Event Configuration
children.description: This section defines the Discovery Controller Asynchronous Event Configuration, detailing mechanisms for managing NVMe-over-Fabrics (NVMe-oF) asynchronous event requests and event reporting. It specifies event types, AEN (Asynchronous Event Notification) handling, and subscription procedures to ensure reliable state-change communication between the controller and host.

children.title: 3.1.3.3.2 Discovery Controller Asynchronous Event Information – Requests and Notifications
children.description: This section defines the constraints and utilization of special Controller IDs (FFF0h–FFFFh) within NVMe-oF Discovery controllers, specifically for static controller allocation and dispersed namespace identification. It addresses which reserved identifiers are prohibited in `Connect` commands and explains how specific values facilitate NVM subsystem attachment and multisession connectivity.

children.title: 3.1.3.3.3 Discovery Controller Initialization
children.description: Discussion of Discovery controller initialization, asynchronous event notifications (Discovery Log Page Change F0h, Host Discovery Log Page Change F1h

children.title: 3.1.3.4 Command Support Requirements
children.description: This section specifies mandatory, optional, and prohibited NVMe command support for I/O, Administrative, and Discovery controllers. It defines technical requirements for Asynchronous Event notifications and controller-specific command capabilities within the NVMe 2.0 framework.

children.title: 3.1.3.5 Log Page Support Requirements
children.description: This section outlines mandatory, optional, and prohibited support requirements for NVMe log pages across I/O, Administrative, and Discovery controllers. It defines the implementation compliance standards for log page identifiers, including Error Information and SMART/Health data, to ensure standardized diagnostic reporting across NVM Express architectures.

children.title: 3.1.3.6 Feature Support Requirements
children.description: Log page support requirements for NVMe Base Spec 2.3, detailing controller support (mandatory, optional, prohibited) for each Log Page Identifier (e.g., Discovery 80h, Reservation Notification 81h, Sanitize Status 81h, Program List 82h, Memory Range Set 84h, Changed Zone List BFh), applicable transport constraints, and dependencies on features such as Fixed Capacity Management, Flexible Data Placement, Reachability Reporting, and NVMe‑MI command support. Answers which log pages must be implemented, may be omitted, or are disallowed under specific controller capabilities, transport specifications, and feature enablement conditions.

children.title: 3.1.4 Controller Properties
children.description: This section defines the mandatory, optional, and prohibited feature support requirements for I/O, Administrative, and Discovery controllers within the NVMe 2.3 specification. It serves as a technical reference to determine which specific features are required for a controller to achieve compliance based on its transport type, NVM subsystem capabilities, and supported advanced functions.

children.title: 3.1.4.1 Offset 0h: CAP – Controller Capabilities
children.description: This section defines Controller Capabilities (CAP) through memory-based and message-based property definitions for I/O, Admin, and Discovery controllers. It utilizes technical terms such as Offset (OFST), Transport Specific (T), and access types (RO, RW, RWC, RWS, HwInit, Impl Spec) to specify memory mapping, register reserved bit handling, and property implementation constraints.

children.title: 3.1.4.3 Offset Ch: INTMS – Interrupt Mask Set
children.description: This section details the NVMe Controller Capabilities (CAP) and Version (VS) properties, specifically defining requirements for physically contiguous queues via the CQR bit and maximum queue limits through the MQES field. It answers technical questions regarding hardware constraints on queue memory allocation and controller-supported specification versions for NVMe over PCIe and Fabrics implementations.

children.title: 3.1.4.4 Offset 10h: INTMC – Interrupt Mask Clear
children.description: This section details the INTMC register at offset 10h, focusing on the bitwise mechanism for clearing specific interrupt masks within the system architecture. It addresses how to programmatically reset pending interrupt enables and identifies which hardware events are governed by this mask-clearing operation.

children.title: 3.1.4.5 Offset 14h: CC – Controller Configuration
children.description: This section defines the Controller Configuration (CC) and Interrupt Masking (INTMS/INTMC) registers within the NVMe Base Specification. It details technical requirements for host-based interrupt management, including vector masking behavior and register accessibility constraints when utilizing MSI-X versus pin-based or MSI transport modes.

children.title: 3.1.4.6 Offset 1Ch: CSTS – Controller Status
children.description: This section defines the **Controller Configuration (CC)** register’s **Enable (EN)** bit, detailing controller reset mechanics, command processing behavior, and state preservation protocols. It addresses how toggling the EN bit triggers a **Controller Level Reset**, manages **I/O Queue** lifecycle, and impacts internal controller configuration values.

children.title: 3.1.4.7 Offset 20h: NSSR – NVM Subsystem Reset
children.description: This section defines the NVM Subsystem Reset (NSSR) register and its role in recovering a controller following fatal errors or shutdown processes. It specifies the mandatory reset procedures and sequence requirements for re-enabling an NVMe controller to resume command execution without resulting in undefined behavior.

children.title: 3.1.4.8 Offset 24h: AQA – Admin Queue Attributes
children.description: This section defines the **Admin Queue Attributes (AQA)** register, detailing bitfield specifications for **Admin Submission Queue (ASQ)** and **Admin Completion Queue (ACQ)** sizes. It answers how host software configures **queue depth** and **queue identifier** parameters during NVM Express initialization.

children.title: 3.1.4.9 Offset 28h: ASQ – Admin Submission Queue Base Address
children.description: This section defines the 64-bit physical memory address for the NVMe Admin Submission Queue (ASQ) base register. It details memory alignment requirements, configuration procedures for the controller, and how the host system initializes command submission via this base address pointer.

children.title: 3.1.4.10 Offset 30h: ACQ – Admin Completion Queue Base Address
children.description: This section defines the NVM Subsystem Reset (NSSR) control register and the Admin Queue Attributes (AQA) register, including technical specifications for Admin Submission and Completion Queue configuration. It answers how to trigger a subsystem reset via a specific 32-bit key and specifies the required memory and attribute constraints for initializing Admin Queues.

children.title: 3.1.4.11 Offset 38h: CMBLOC – Controller Memory Buffer Location
children.description: Offset 38h CMBLOC defines the Controller Memory Buffer location, specifying the buffer’s offset (bits 31‑12) in units of CMBSZ, its dword‑alignment flag (CQDA, bit 8), and reserved fields, and answers how software determines the physical address of the optional CMB when CAP.CMBS is set and CMBMSC.CRE is cleared.

children.title: 3.1.4.12 Offset 3Ch: CMBSZ – Controller Memory Buffer Size
children.description: This section details the **CMBSZ (Controller Memory Buffer Size)** register, defining the memory capacity and offset address for the **NVMe Controller Memory Buffer (CMB)**. It specifies the units (4KB granularity) and capabilities required to determine if a controller supports host-accessible memory for data transfers.

children.title: 3.1.4.13 Offset 40h: BPINFO – Boot Partition Information
children.description: This section defines the Controller Memory Buffer Size (CMBSZ) property, detailing its capacity units, granularities, and Write Data Support (WDS) functionality. It addresses how the NVM Express controller reports available memory buffer size and specifies constraints for host-to-controller data transfers within the Controller Memory Buffer.

children.title: 3.1.4.14 Offset 44h: BPRSEL – Boot Partition Read Select
children.description: This section defines the **BPRSEL (Boot Partition Read Select) register** located at **offset 44h**, detailing its bit-field configuration for managing **active boot partition access**. It explains how the register controls the memory mapping of firmware partitions and specifies the mechanism for selecting the designated read-only or read-write partition during the system boot sequence.

children.title: 3.1.4.15 Offset 48h: BPMBL – Boot Partition Memory Buffer Location
children.description: This section defines the **BPMBL** (Boot Partition Memory Buffer Location) and **BPINFO** (Boot Partition Information) registers, detailing key parameters such as the **Active Boot Partition ID (ABPID)**, **Boot Read Status (BRS)**, and **Boot Partition Size (BPSZ)**. It specifies how these fields govern the status tracking and memory buffer mapping for NVMe Boot Partition read operations initiated via the **BPRSEL** register.

children.title: 3.1.4.16 Offset 50h: CMBMSC – Controller Memory Buffer Memory Space Control
children.description: This section defines the Controller Memory Buffer Memory Space Control (CMBMSC) register, detailing bit-level configurations for Controller Memory Buffer (CMB) location, size, and status. It answers how software discovers, enables, and manages the operational memory parameters of the NVMe controller's memory buffer space.

children.title: 3.1.4.17 Offset 58h: CMBSTS – Controller Memory Buffer Status
children.description: This section defines the **CMBMSC (Controller Memory Buffer Memory Space Control)** register, detailing the **Controller Base Address (CBA)** configuration and **Controller Memory Space Enable (CMSE)** bit for NVMe Controller Memory Buffers. It specifies address alignment requirements, non-volatile reset behavior, and coexistence constraints with Persistent Memory Regions.

children.title: 3.1.4.18 Offset 5Ch: CMBEBS – Controller Memory Buffer Elasticity Buffer Size
children.description: This section defines the Controller Memory Buffer Elasticity Buffer Size (CMBEBS) register, specifying the units and capacity constraints for memory elasticity within the NVMe Controller Memory Buffer. It identifies whether the controller supports adaptive buffer sizing and details the protocol requirements for host-side memory management.

children.title: 3.1.4.19 Offset 60h: CMBSWTP – Controller Memory Buffer Sustained Write Throughput
children.description: This section details the

children.title: 3.1.4.20 Offset 64h: NSSD – NVM Subsystem Shutdown
children.description: This section details the NVM Subsystem Shutdown (NSSD) register at offset 64h, specifying the protocol for initiating controlled power-down sequences within the NVM subsystem. It addresses how to trigger system shutdown, manage non-volatile memory states, and ensure data integrity during power transition events.

children.title: 3.1.4.21 Offset 68h: CRTO – Controller Ready Timeouts
children.description: This section details the NSSD (Offset 64h) NVM Subsystem Shutdown property, covering its support indication (CAP.NSSS), shutdown control values (“Nrml”

children.title: 3.1.4.22 Offset E00h: PMRCAP – Persistent Memory Region Capabilities
children.description: This section defines the **PMRCAP (Persistent Memory Region Capabilities)** register, detailing the technical features required to identify and manage persistent memory support in NVMe controllers. It answers what specific capabilities a device exposes for persistent memory regions and specifies that this register remains persistent across controller-level resets.

children.title: 3.1.4.23 Offset E04h: PMRCTL – Persistent Memory Region Control
children.description: This section specifies the **PMRCTL** register bit-fields controlling **Persistent Memory Region (PMR)** enable status and error handling. It documents how to configure **NVMe memory-mapped access** and defines the interface for managing byte-addressable persistent storage state.

children.title: 3.1.4.24 Offset E08h: PMRSTS – Persistent Memory Region Status
children.description: Provides detailed definitions of PMRSTS fields, including Persistent Memory Region time units (PMRTU), Base Indicator

children.title: 3.1.4.25 Offset E0Ch: PMREBS – Persistent Memory Region Elasticity Buffer Size
children.description: This section details the PMRSTS (Offset E08h) register fields—CBAI (bit 12) indicating controller base‑address validity, and HSTS (bits 11:9) defining Persistent Memory Region health states (Normal, Restore Error, Read‑Only) with associated conditions and reset behavior, answering how to interpret status bits, when they are set or cleared, and the impact of PMRCAP.CMSS and PMRMSCL.CMSE on validity checks.

children.title: 3.1.4.26 Offset E10h: PMRSWTP – Persistent Memory Region Sustained Write Throughput
children.description: This section defines the **PMRSWTP register** within the **Persistent Memory Region (PMR)**, detailing the **Sustained Write Throughput** metric. It specifies the field definition, bit-level encoding, and reporting requirements necessary to determine the maximum reliable write bandwidth for persistent memory devices.

children.title: 3.1.4.27 Offset E14h: PMRMSCL – Persistent Memory Region Memory Space Control Lower
children.description: This section defines the Persistent Memory Region (PMR) Elasticity Buffer’s operational parameters, including the buffer size base (PMRWBZ), unit granularity (PMRSZU), and read bypass behavior (PMRRBB). It clarifies how a host determines the physical capacity and access ordering requirements for high-performance memory writes within an NVMe-oF or local NVM subsystem.

children.title: 3.1.4.28 Offset E18h: PMRMSCU – Persistent Memory Region Memory Space Control Upper
children.description: This section details the PMRMSCU (Offset E18h) Persistent Memory

children.title: 3.2 NVM Subsystem Entities
children.description: Section 3.2 defines the architectural hierarchy of NVM subsystem entities, including Namespaces, NVM Sets, Endurance Groups, Reclaim Units, and Domains. This section clarifies how NVMe storage resources are partitioned, managed, and organized to address questions regarding data isolation, endurance management, and capacity reclamation.

children.title: 3.2.1 Namespaces
children.description: Section 3.2.1 defines NVMe Namespace Identifiers (NSIDs), covering their lifecycle, allocation requirements, and status classifications. It provides technical specifications for managing NSID validity, namespace-to-controller mapping, and the association of namespaces with specific I/O command sets.

children.title: 3.2.1.1 Namespace Overview
children.description: NVMe “Persistent Memory Region Memory Space Control Lower (PMRMSCL) 0xE14” register layout, fields, and reset

children.title: 3.2.1.5 NSID and Namespace Relationships
children.description: This section defines NSID validity rules—including allocated vs. unallocated NSIDs, active vs. inactive NSIDs per controller, and the broadcast NSID (FFFFFFFFh)—and answers which NSID values are permissible, how namespace attachment status is represented, and how controllers interpret NSID ranges within the Identify Controller data structure.

children.title: 3.2.1.6 NSID and Namespace Usage
children.description: This section defines Namespace Identifier (NSID) uniqueness requirements, allocation scope, and operational constraints within an NVM subsystem. It specifies whether NSIDs must be globally consistent across controllers based on supported capabilities, such as Namespace Management, ANA Reporting, or NVM Sets, and clarifies how the NN and MNAN fields determine namespace limits.

children.title: 3.2.1.7 I/O Command Set Associations
children.description: This section details methods for identifying and tracking allocated Namespace IDs (NSIDs) within an NVM subsystem using the Identify command (CNS 10h). It defines technical requirements for persisting namespace identity across power cycles via UUID, NGUID, and EUI64 descriptors, while addressing unique identifier reuse through the NSFEAT field and Asymmetric Namespace Access Reporting dependencies.

children.title: 3.2.2 NVM Sets
children.description: This section details mechanisms for host-side enumeration and identification of allocated Namespace IDs (NSIDs) within an NVM subsystem using Identify command CNS 10h. It addresses how to maintain namespace persistence across power cycles through UUID, NGUID, and EUI64 descriptors, while referencing Asymmetric Namespace Access (ANA) reporting and UIDREUSE characteristics.

children.title: 3.2.3 Endurance Groups
children.description: This section defines NVM Set identification, management, and their strict hierarchical association with Endurance Groups within an NVM subsystem. It specifies requirements for NVM Set attribute inheritance during namespace creation, administrative command validation, and identifying support in controller attributes.

children.title: 3.2.3.1 Configuring and Managing Endurance Group Events
children.description: This section outlines infrastructure requirements and configuration protocols for NVM Express Endurance Group event monitoring. children.title: 3.6 Shutdown Processing
children.description: This section details NVM Express shutdown protocols, specifically distinguishing between controller shutdown and NVM Subsystem Shutdown mechanisms triggered via the CC.SHN field or NSSD property. Topics include state monitoring through CSTS.ST and CSTS.SHST status bits, procedural overrides, and eligibility requirements for multi-controller NVM subsystems.

children.title: 3.6.1 Memory-based Controller Shutdown (PCIe)
children.description: This section mandates procedures for safely powering down NVMe controllers and domains by monitoring the **Controller Status (CSTS.SHST)** shutdown status field. It defines implementation requirements based on **Controller Power Scope (CAP.CPS)**—specifically **Controller, Domain, and NVM subsystem scopes**—to ensure data integrity during power removal and outlines interactions with **NVMe-MI out-of-band management** mechanisms.

children.title: 3.6.2 Message-based Controller Shutdown (Fabrics)
children.description: This section details NVMe controller shutdown readiness, covering CSTS.ST bit, CSTS.SHST field, CC.EN bit, Controller Level Reset, Commands Aborted due to Power Loss Notification, out‑of‑band admin commands, and the Ignore Shutdown bit. It answers when a controller is ready for power‑off, how resets or admin commands affect the media shutdown state, and which status codes are issued during shutdown processing.

children.title: 3.6.3 NVM Subsystem Shutdown
children.description: This section outlines the **NVM Subsystem Shutdown** procedure, detailing the **CSTS.SHST** polling requirements, host-initiated command aborts, and post-shutdown **controller state** limitations. It addresses how to ensure data integrity during shutdown and defines the specific constraints on a controller's behavior, such as **Keep Alive timer** suspension and exclusive **Fabrics command** processing, before a **Controller Level Reset**.

children.title: 3.6.3.1 NVM Subsystem Shutdown in a Single Domain NVM Subsystem
children.description: This section defines NVM Subsystem Shutdown behavior in single-domain architectures, contrasting functional differences between `CAP.NSSES` bit states (1 vs. 0). It details how `CAP.NSSES` configurations impact Controller Level Reset (CLR) availability, `CSTS.ST`, and `CSTS.SHST` persistence during shutdown processes.

children.title: 3.6.3.2 Domain Shutdown in a Multiple Domain NVM Subsystem
children.description: This section defines the operational requirements and state transitions for NVM Subsystem Shutdowns within multi-domain architectures. Key topics include `CSTS.SHST` status flags, `Ignore Shutdown` command execution via OOB management, and how NVM Subsystem Resets abort or clear active shutdown states across all participating controllers.

children.title: 3.7 Resets
children.description: This section details NVMe reset mechanisms—including NVM Subsystem, Controller, and I/O Queue-level resets—governed by technical specifications for state restoration and error recovery. It defines the operational scope, triggering conditions, and recovery behaviors necessary to resolve hardware faults or reinitialize NVMe controller environments.

children.title: 3.7.1 NVM Subsystem Reset
children.description: This section details the NVM Subsystem Reset process, focusing on the architectural distinction and functional requirements for Single Domain and Multiple Domain configurations. It addresses how NVM subsystems manage reset sequences, control authority, and state initialization across different hardware implementations.

children.title: 3.7.1.2 Multiple Domain NVM Subsystems
children.description: This section explains NVM Subsystem Reset behavior in single‑domain NVMe subsystems, outlining reset triggers (main‑power apply, NSSR.NSSRC = 0x4E564D65, Management Interface request, vendor‑specific events), scope (entire subsystem reset, controller‑level resets, Persistent Memory Region disable, transport‑specific actions), and host detection via the CSTS.NSSRO field. It answers how and when a full subsystem reset occurs, what components are affected, and how the host distinguishes a subsystem reset from other communication losses.

children.title: 3.7.2 Controller Level Reset
children.description: This section defines the Controller Level Reset (CLR) mechanism within the NVMe 2.3 specification, detailing methods such as NVM Subsystem Resets, CC.EN transitions, and Cross-Controller Reset commands. It outlines mandatory controller actions during a reset, including queue deletion, state initialization, and the clearing of the CSTS.RDY bit, clarifying how host-initiated resets and transport-specific events return a controller to an idle, reset state.

children.title: 3.7.2.1 Controller Reset
children.description: This section outlines NVMe controller reset behavior, focusing on state transitions of the CC.EN and CSTS flags, message-based controller association persistence, and host communication requirements during reset sequences. It defines recovery protocols, the preservation of Admin Queue connections, and safety recommendations for hosts losing communication during pending command completion or device readiness transitions.

children.title: 3.7.3 Queue Level Reset of I/O Queues
children.description: This section outlines NVMe I/O Queue behavior during Queue Level Resets (CLR) and Controller Resets, specifically addressing host-controller communication loss, association persistence, and Fabrics command handling. It defines recovery requirements for message-based controllers and specifies host responsibilities when managing outstanding command completions and Controller Ready (CSTS.RDY) status during reset events.

children.title: 3.8 NVM Capacity Model
children.description: This section details the NVM Capacity Model, covering media unit organization, logical-to-physical mapping, and standardized capacity reporting mechanisms. children.title: 3.8.2 Media Unit Organization Examples
children.description: This section details the hierarchical NVM capacity management model, including relationships between NVM subsystems, domains, Endurance Groups, NVM Sets, namespaces, and Media Units. It defines capacity allocation rules, specifies how storage entities are organized within the controller, and addresses how capacity is partitioned from Endurance Groups down to individual namespaces.

children.title: 3.8.2.2 Vertically-Organized NVM Subsystem

children.title: 3.8.2.3 Horizontally-Organized Dual NAND NVM Subsystem

It answers how NVMe systems implement multi-mode NAND storage (SLC vs. QLC) to optimize bandwidth through specific Capacity Adjustment Factors and channel-level resource allocation.

children.title: 3.8.3 Capacity Reporting
children.description: This section defines NVM Express capacity reporting architecture, detailing how "Endurance Group Configuration Descriptors," "NVM Sets," and "Media Unit Configuration Descriptors" structure partitioned subsystem storage. It explains how "TNVMCAP," "UNVMCAP," and "MEGCAP" fields quantify available capacity and defines the reporting requirements for NVM subsystems with and without multiple domain support.

children.title: 3.9 Keep Alive
children.description: Section 3.9 Keep Alive details host use of the Get Log Page command for Endurance Group Information, the Identify command for NVM Set List access, and the mapping of capacity‑information fields across NVM Sets, Endurance Groups, and domains to answer how a host determines available capacity and how controllers adjust capacity during creation or deletion of these entities.

children.title: 3.9.3 Command Based Keep Alive
children.description: This section defines the Command-Based Keep Alive mechanism for maintaining communication stability between the Controller and Host. It addresses technical implementation requirements, synchronization protocols, and configuration parameters necessary to troubleshoot and manage active session persistence.

children.title: 3.9.3.1 Command Based Keep Alive on the Controller
children.description: This section defines requirements for the Keep Alive Timer feature in NVMe Transports, covering configuration via Fabric Connect or Set Features commands and error handling for invalid timeout values. It addresses how controllers enforce minimum/maximum timeout constraints and manage command aborts when Keep Alive configurations violate transport-specific specifications.

children.title: 3.9.3.2 Command Based Keep Alive on the Host
children.description: This section defines the operational logic for the NVMe Command Based Keep Alive mechanism, specifying conditions for timer activation, restarts via Keep Alive or Set Features commands, and timeout triggers. It outlines protocol requirements for host-side command frequency to mitigate transport latency and prevent controller-side cleanup actions.

children.title: 3.9.4 Traffic Based Keep Alive
children.description: This section details Traffic Based Keep Alive mechanisms, covering heartbeat monitoring, session persistence, and timeout management for both Controller and Host architectures. It addresses how to maintain active connections and detect link failures through data-plane traffic analysis.

children.title: 3.9.4.1 Traffic Based Keep Alive on the Controller
children.description: This section explains NVM Express Keep Alive Timer handling (Feature Identifier 0Fh) by detailing KATT/KATT‑half calculations, Keep‑Alive Send Timer start, restart, stop, expiration, and timeout detection, and clarifies how Traffic‑Based Keep‑Alive on a controller prevents Keep‑Alive Timeouts during ongoing Admin or I/O command traffic.

children.title: 3.9.4.2 Traffic Based Keep Alive on the Host
children.description: This section details the **Traffic Based Keep Alive** mechanism, specifying the operational requirements and host-side check intervals necessary to maintain connectivity with an NVM Express® controller. It answers how hosts can optimize command overhead by replacing explicit Keep Alive commands with routine I/O or Admin traffic while adjusting the check frequency to KATT/4 to avoid false Keep Alive Timeouts.

children.title: 3.9.5 Keep Alive Timeout Cleanup
children.description: This section details the state machine and timing logic for the host-side Keep Alive Send Timer within the NVMe Keep Alive mechanism. It defines the specific conditions for enabling, starting, restarting, and stopping the timer, providing technical requirements for calculating the timer duration based on KATT/4 and the previous expiry timestamp.

children.title: 3.11 Firmware Update Process

children.description: Section 4.2 defines the Completion Queue Entry (CQE) structure, covering common fields for Admin, I/O, and Fabrics commands alongside Status Field encodings and Phase Tag synchronization. children.description: This section defines the 16-byte common Completion Queue Entry (CQE) structure and the Fabrics Command Dword 0 layout within the NVMe 2.3 specification. It establishes technical requirements for Command Identifiers, SGL-based data transfer protocols (PSDT), and opcode assignments to define mandatory command-specific Dword reporting.

children.description: Routing description: This section defines the NVMe Completion Queue Entry (CQE) status field, detailing the 31‑17 STATUS bits,

children.title: 4.2.3.1 Generic Command Status Definition
children.description: This section defines Generic Command Status values for NVMe 2.3 completion queue entries, specifically addressing status codes 00h through 04h including successful completion, invalid opcodes, field errors, command ID conflicts, and data transfer failures. It functions as a technical reference for interpreting generic command-level error results across various NVM Express I/O command sets.

children.title: 4.2.3.2 Command Specific Status Definition
children.description: This section defines Command Specific Status values (2Ah–84h) within the NVMe 2.3 specification, covering error conditions such as invalid Placement Handles, sanitize operation failures, LBA range violations, and reservation conflicts. It provides the technical criteria for command aborts and status interpretations to assist developers in troubleshooting namespace state transitions and command eligibility.

children.title: 4.2.3.3 Media and Data Integrity Errors Definition
children.description: This section defines media and data integrity errors, addressing fault mechanisms such as latent sector errors, bit rot, and checksum mismatches. It explains how storage systems detect, report, and recover from hardware-level corruption and persistent data loss.

children.title: 4.2.3.4 Path Related Status Definition
children.description: Path‑Related Status Definition (NVMe 2.3) details the SCT = 0x02 (Command‑Specific) fabric status ranges (B0h‑BFh) referencing transport‑specific bindings, and enumerates Media and Data Integrity error codes (80h‑85h) such as Write Fault, Unrecovered Read Error, End‑to‑End Guard/Application/Reference Tag Check Errors, and Compare Failure, answering how NVMe‑Fabrics and generic NVM commands report media‑integrity failures and which commands are affected.

children.title: 4.2.4 Phase Tag
children.description: This section defines NVMe Path Related Status values, detailing error handling protocols such as Internal Path Errors and Asymmetric Namespace Access (ANA) states, including Persistent Loss, Inaccessible, and Transition. It specifies whether commands should be retried across different paths or if controller resubmission is prohibited based on the specific path-related failure.

children.title: 4.2.4.1 Phase Tag Example
children.description: This section illustrates the NVMe Completion Queue Phase Tag mechanism through a six-slot buffer example, detailing how Phase Tag (P) bits toggle during controller command completion and host consumption cycles. It clarifies the operational logic behind Head and Tail pointer tracking and the synchronization of queue state transitions to prevent command processing errors.

children.title: 4.3 Data Pointer Layouts (PRPs and SGLs)
children.description: This section details NVMe data transfer mechanisms through Physical Region Page (PRP) entries and Scatter Gather Lists (SGLs), including Metadata Region (MR) structures. It addresses how host software defines non-contiguous memory layouts and specifies memory address pointers required for direct memory access (DMA) operations between the controller and host.

children.title: 4.3.1 Physical Region Page Entry and List
children.description: This section details the mechanical operation of NVMe Completion Queue (CQ) pointer management, specifically addressing status updates for the Head and Tail Pointers during command completion and host consumption. It defines operational states such as queue wrapping via Phase Tag inversion and the transition into a "Full" queue condition.

children.title: 4.3.2 Scatter Gather List (SGL)
children.description: This section defines Scatter Gather Lists (SGLs) as memory data structures used for describing source or destination data buffers in NVMe command transfers. It details SGL segment composition, specifies requirements for SGL length parity with requested transfer lengths, and outlines error handling protocols when SGLs under-describe required data buffer sizes.

children.title: 4.3.2.1 SGL Example
children.description: This section details the structure and byte-level definitions of Keyed and Transport SGL Data Block descriptors within the NVMe 2.3 specification. It answers how to correctly configure SGL Identifier (SGLID) fields, define data block lengths, and adhere to transport-specific alignment requirements for NVMe data transfer operations.

children.title: 4.3.3 Metadata Region (MR)
children.description: This section defines the Metadata Region (MR) as a command-set-specific specification within the NVMe architecture and introduces the Feature Values mechanism. It explains how controller operating parameters are managed, queried, and modified using Get Features and Set Features commands identified by Feature Identifiers.

children.title: 4.4 Feature Values
children.description: Section 4.4 details the management of controller operating parameters using Get Features and Set Features commands, which employ Feature Identifiers to group and modify specific attributes. This section answers how host software can programmatically read or alter NVMe controller behavior by identifying and accessing these configurable feature sets.

children.title: 4.5 Identifier Format and Layout (Informative)
children.description: Section 4.5 defines standard schema formats and structural layouts for hardware and non-volatile memory identifiers, including PCI VIDs, IEEE OUIs, EUI-64, NGUIDs, and UUIDs. This section addresses how to uniquely identify storage devices and controllers while detailing the technical specifications for vendor-assigned serial numbers, model numbers, and globally unique identification strings.

children.title: 4.5.1 PCI Vendor ID (VID) and PCI Subsystem Vendor ID (SSVID)
children.description: This section details the identification structure of PCI devices, specifically defining the Vendor ID (VID) and Subsystem Vendor ID (SSVID) registers. It explains how these unique 16-bit identifiers are assigned by the PCI-SIG to ensure hardware interoperability and clarifies how the system distinguishes between silicon manufacturers and equipment OEMs.

children.title: 4.5.3 IEEE OUI Identifier (IEEE)
children.description: This section details NVMe specification requirements for Namespace ID (NSID) handling in Get/Set Features commands and provides implementation guidance for PCI Vendor ID (VID) and Subsystem Vendor ID (SSVID) formats. It clarifies controller command-abort conditions based on feature scope and defines the structure for 16-bit little-endian PCI identifiers.

children.title: 4.5.5 Namespace Globally Unique Identifier (NGUID)
children.description: This section defines the Namespace Globally Unique Identifier (NGUID) and the IEEE Extended Unique Identifier (EUI64) format within the NVMe 2.3 specification. It explains the structural mapping of MA-L identifiers, detailing byte-level organization and big-endian formatting for OUI and Extension Identifier fields to ensure namespace uniqueness.

children.title: 4.5.6 Universally Unique Identifier (UUID)
children.description: This section details the construction and byte-level formatting of the Namespace Globally Unique Identifier (NGUID) and its structural alignment with the IEEE Registered Extended designator (NAA=6) World Wide Name (WWN). It answers how NVMe namespaces uniquely identify storage resources using specific OUI, Extension Identifier, and Vendor Specific fields in accordance with RFC 9562 UUID standards.

children.title: 4.6 List Data Structures
children.description: This section details the architectural implementation of controller and namespace list data structures used for organizational mapping and resource management. It addresses how these structures index system entities and provides technical specifications required for efficient retrieval within the API framework.

children.title: 4.6.1 Controller List
children.description: This section defines the structural specifications for the Namespace Globally Unique Identifier (NGUID) and Universally Unique Identifier (UUID) within the NVM Express architecture. It details byte-level mappings for Organizationally Unique Identifiers (OUI) and vendor-specific extensions, explaining how these fields align with World Wide Name (WWN) IEEE Registered Extended designators.

children.title: 4.6.2 Namespace List
children.description: This section defines the structural format of Controller and Namespace Lists within the NVMe 2.3 specification, detailing byte-level data arrangements for controller identifiers and namespace IDs. It provides the technical specifications necessary to interpret list length, handle empty fields, and determine the ordering protocols for identifying NVM subsystem resources.

children.title: 4.7 NVMe Qualified Names
children.description: This section details the NVMe Qualified Name (QNAME) structures by specifying the Controller List format (NUMCIDS field, per‑controller unique identifiers, max 2,047 entries) and the Namespace List format (ordered 32‑bit namespace IDs, zero‑filled unused entries, size calculation N × 4 + 3), answering how to locate, count, and interpret controller and namespace identifiers within a QNAME.

children.title: 5 Admin Command Set
