It addresses how the network tracks device availability and manages signaling flows to ensure efficient reachability state synchronization between the UE and core network functions.

children.title: 8.1.21.1 Reachability Reporting overview
children.description: Section 8.1.21.1 Reachability Reporting overview covers Predictable Latency Mode, Deterministic Window (DTWIN) and Non‑Deterministic Window

children.title: 8.1.21.2 Reachability event notifications
children.description: This section details NVMe ® Reachability event notifications, explaining how Reachability Groups (

children.title: 8.1.22 Read Recovery Level
children.description: This section details **Reachability Group and Reachability Association Change Notices** within the NVMe specification, covering event triggers for host log page updates. It defines technical criteria for NSID and RGID management, identifying when a host must query the **Reachability Groups log page** to reflect controller-side configuration changes.

children.title: 8.1.23 Replay Protected Memory Block
children.description: This section defines the Replay Protected Memory Block (RPMB) as an authenticated, anti-replay storage facility for NVM Express devices. It details security procedures including shared secret authentication key programming, Message Authentication Code (MAC) generation, nonces, and write counters to ensure secure data access.

children.title: 8.1.23.2 RPMB Operations
children.description: This section covers Replay Protected Memory Block (RPMB) operations, specifically detailing procedures for authentication key provisioning, monotonic write counter management, and secure authenticated data read/write transactions. It provides specifications for ensuring data integrity and anti-replay protection within eMMC/UFS storage subsystems.

children.title: 8.1.23.2.1 Authentication Key Programming
children.description: This section details the HMAC SHA-256 authentication procedure and RPMB data frame construction for the NVMe non-volatile memory block. It specifies requirements for Security Send and Security Receive command sequences used to program authentication keys and manage authenticated memory access.

children.title: 8.1.23.2.3 Authenticated Data Write
children.description: This section outlines the NVMe RPMB authenticated write procedures, detailing the structure of security commands, MAC generation, and the write counter synchronization flow. It provides technical requirements for implementing secure data commit sequences between the host and the controller to ensure valid RPMB memory access.

children.title: 8.1.23.2.4 Authenticated Data Read
children.description: This section details the formal protocol flow for RPMB Authenticated Data Write operations, specifying byte-level command structures for requests, result verification, and host-controller communication. It defines the required MAC, Nonce, Write Counter, and data frame parameters to ensure secure, authenticated persistence within NVMe Replay Protected Memory Blocks.

children.title: 8.1.23.3 Authenticated Device Configuration Block Write
children.description: This section defines the **Authenticated Device Configuration Block Write** procedure within the **NVMe RPMB** framework, detailing the **Security Send** command structure, **Request Message Type 0006h**, and authentication requirements. It specifies the necessary **MAC**, **Write Counter**, and **Data Frame** field configurations required to securely update device-specific configuration blocks.

children.title: 8.1.23.4 Authenticated Device Configuration Block Read
children.description: This section details the **Authenticated Device Configuration Block (DCB) Read** process within the NVMe 2.3 specification, focusing on the independence of **RPMB** target write counters from DCB operations. It specifically addresses how to securely verify the **Write Counter** and manage the data integrity of the **Device Configuration Block** structure during authenticated firmware or configuration updates.

children.title: 8.1.24 Reservations
children.description: RPMB authenticated Device Configuration Block read flow, covering command/response field layout, MAC calculation, Write Counter separation, Nonce handling, and Result codes 0001h (general failure) and 0002h (authentication failure); answers how the host constructs the read request, how the controller validates and returns the block, and what error conditions are signaled.

children.title: 8.1.24.1 Reservation Types
children.description: This section details NVM Express (NVMe) reservation support requirements, specifically governing **Identify Namespace (RESCAP)** and **Identify Controller (ONCS)** field configurations. It outlines mandatory error handling procedures for **non-supported namespaces or controllers** and clarifies that while **NVM subsystem controllers** must share identical reservation capabilities, individual namespaces may vary.

children.title: 8.1.24.2 Reservation Notifications
children.description: This section defines the mandatory "Reservation Conflict" status code responses for NVMe commands based on specific reservation types—including Write Exclusive and Exclusive Access—and the host's registrant status. It establishes the operational constraints for I/O and Administrative commands when acting under active persistent reservations, determining whether command execution is allowed or prohibited.

children.title: 8.1.24.4 Unregistering
children.description: This section defines the process of becoming a namespace registrant by establishing a reservation key within an NVMe subsystem. It covers Host Identifiers, registration persistence across associated controllers, and dispersed namespace access requirements.

children.title: 8.1.24.7 Preempting a Reservation or Registration
children.description: This section details the NVMe reservation lifecycle, specifically the conditions for unregistering a host, the automatic release of reservations, and the procedural requirements for acquiring a reservation via the `Reservation Acquire` command. It addresses how host registration status affects reservation persistence and the necessary prerequisites for a host to secure exclusive namespace access.

children.title: 8.1.24.9 Reporting Reservation Status
children.description: This section defines the mandatory synchronous completion requirements for **Reservation Acquire** commands with the **Preempt** action, specifying that the controller must ensure all commands targeted at the preempted host are aborted or completed before returning a status. It clarifies the best-effort nature of these abort requests and the procedural dependencies between **namespace reservation state changes** and IO command termination.

children.title: 8.1.25 Rotational Media
children.description: This section explains the behavior of the Reservation Acquire command with RACQA = 001b (Preempt), detailing how atomic namespace reservation changes trigger abort requests to all controllers of preempted hosts, the required completion ordering of abort‑requested commands before the Reservation Acquire finishes, and the best‑effort nature of such aborts; it answers how preemptive reservation acquisition affects in‑flight commands, abort semantics, and controller coordination per NVMe 2.3 specifications.

children.title: 8.1.26 Sanitize Operations
children.description: This section defines requirements for NVM Express controllers managing rotational media, specifically addressing Namespace/Endurance Group feature bits, Spinup Control behavior, and operational power states. It provides technical guidance for ensuring rotational media compliance during NVM Subsystem Resets and identifying when controllers must remain in non-operational power states.

children.title: 8.1.26.1 Common Requirements For Commands Requesting Sanitize Operations
children.description: Section 8.1.26.1 defines shared operational behaviors for NVMe Sanitize and Sanitize Namespace commands, covering background execution, status monitoring, and asynchronous event notifications. It addresses requirements for initiating sanitization, managing post-operation states like Media Verification, and reporting results via the Sanitize Status log.

children.title: 8.1.26.2 Elements of Sanitize Operations
children.description: This section defines the constraints and procedural requirements for initiating **NVM Express sanitize operations**, specifically addressing status code handling for **pending firmware activation**, **personality setting changes**, and **SMART/Health Critical Warnings**. It explains how the controller manages background execution and command abort conditions when concurrent administrative tasks conflict with data sanitization.

children.title: 8.1.26.3 Sanitize Operation Types and Support
children.description: This section defines the mandatory controller states—Restricted Processing, Unrestricted Processing, and Post-Verification Deallocation—required for executing media deallocation during NVM Express sanitize operations. It specifies the operational state transitions governed by the Enter Media Verification State (EMVS), Allow Unrestricted Sanitize (AUSE), and No-Deallocate After Sanitize (NDAS) configuration bits.

children.title: 8.1.26.3.1 Sanitize NVM Subsystem Failure Handling
children.description: This section defines error recovery protocols for Sanitize operations, covering NVM subsystem failure states, abort procedures, and status reporting mechanisms. It establishes the mandatory controller behaviors and state transitions required when a Sanitize process fails to complete successfully across the non-volatile memory media.

children.title: 8.1.26.3.2 Sanitize Namespace Failure Handling
children.description: This section defines failure recovery protocols for Namespace Sanitize operations, specifically detailing restricted controller behavior and I/O command abort requirements when a namespace enters a Restricted or Unrestricted Failure state. It mandates the use of the "Sanitize Namespace Failed" status code for impacted Admin and I/O commands to maintain system integrity during sanitize operation errors.

children.title: 8.1.26.4 Sanitize Operation State Machine
children.description: This section defines the Sanitize Operation State Machine behavior, focusing on failure recovery protocols for NVM subsystems and namespaces. It details specific controller response requirements, including command abort criteria, error status codes (Sanitize Failed, Sanitize Namespace Failed), and the transition procedures necessary to exit Restricted or Unrestricted Failure states.

children.title: 8.1.26.4.1 Idle State
children.description: This section defines the **Idle State** of the **NVMe Sanitize** process, specifying asynchronous event reporting requirements, including **Log Page Identifier 81h** and **Event Specific Parameters**. It clarifies controller behavior when no sanitize operation has occurred, identifying the status signaling standards and internal state field updates required by the **NVM Express Base Specification**.

children.title: 8.1.26.4.2 Restricted Processing State
children.description: This section defines the **Restricted Processing State** within the NVMe sanitization lifecycle, detailing transition requirements for the **Sanitize Status log page**, including **SPROG** and **MVCNCLD** flag management. It specifies controller obligations regarding **media deallocation**, **media modification** requirements, and failure handling when **NVM subsystem composition changes** or **Controller Level Resets** occur.

children.title: 8.1.26.4.3 Restricted Failure State
children.description: This section defines the **Restricted Failure State** during NVMe sanitize operations, outlining conditions for entry following process failures or deallocation errors. It details required NVM subsystem behaviors, mandatory command abort criteria, and the host-driven recovery protocols necessary to clear restricted completion modes via subsequent **Sanitize** or **Sanitize Namespace** commands.

children.title: 8.1.26.4.4 Unrestricted Processing State
children.description: Unrestricted Processing State (NVMe 2.3 §

children.title: 8.1.26.4.5 Unrestricted Failure State
children.description: Unrestricted Failure State: controller behavior after sanitize processing fails in the Unrestricted Processing state, including async event reporting, command abort rules for SANACT values, handling of media verification and deallocation failures, and impact on all controllers and Management Endpoints in the NVM subsystem. Answers how the spec defines error handling, command validation, and state transitions for Sanitize and Sanitize Namespace commands when a sanitize operation enters the Unrestricted Failure State.

children.title: 8.1.26.4.6 Media Verification State
children.description: This section defines the **Media Verification State** within the **NVM Express Sanitize Operation State Machine**, detailing state transitions and log field resets following successful sanitization. It specifies requirements for **NVM subsystem verification**, controller reporting for **Sanitize Progress (SPROG)** and **Media Verification Canceled (MVCNCLD)**, and transitions to **Post-Verification Deallocation** or **Idle** upon failure.

children.title: 8.1.26.4.7 Post-Verification Deallocation State
children.description: This section defines the mandatory "Post-Verification Deallocation" transit conditions and controller actions during an NVM Express sanitize operation. It addresses the technical requirements for updating the Sanitize Status log page, clearing progress fields, and setting the Media Verification Canceled (MVCNCLD) bit following subsystem resets or verification interruptions.

children.title: 8.1.26.5 Sanitize Operation Restrictions
children.description: This section defines the operational constraints and reporting requirements for controllers during NVM subsystem sanitize states, including Restricted/Unrestricted Processing and Post-Verification Deallocation. It specifies requirements for asynchronous event reporting, Sanitize Status log updates, and the suspension of autonomous power state management to ensure secure sanitization protocol compliance.

children.title: 8.1.26.6 Sanitize Operation Effects on Exported NVM Subsystems
children.description: This section defines the scope and behavioral requirements for executing sanitize operations across layered storage architectures featuring Underlying and Exported NVM Subsystems. It addresses how a sanitize process on an Underlying NVM Subsystem impacts data integrity in associated Exported Namespaces and specifies enforced I/O command restrictions during active or failed sanitize operations.

children.title: 8.1.27 Submission Queue (SQ) Associations
children.description: This section defines the mandatory I/O command restrictions enforced on Exported NVM Subsystems when associated Underlying NVM Subsystems are undergoing or recovering from sanitize operations. It addresses how data sanitization propagates across namespace associations and specifies the compliance requirements for Exported Namespaces during these critical maintenance states.

children.title: 8.1.29 Telemetry
children.description: Section 8.1.29 outlines NVMe 2.3 telemetry, specifying vendor‑specific command format (Figure 82), Identify Controller support flags (Admin Vendor Specific Command Configuration, I/O Command Set Vendor Specific Command Configuration), Telemetry Host‑Initiated and Controller‑Initiated log pages (5.2.12.1.8/5.2.12.1.9), Data Area 4 sizing in Log Page Attributes, and the effect of telemetry operation on Predictable Latency. It answers how telemetry is enabled, where support is reported, how hosts validate buffer sizes, and which log pages deliver vendor‑specific diagnostic data.

children.title: 8.1.29.1 Telemetry Data Collection Examples (Informative)
children.description: Telemetry data collection workflow, controller‑initiated log notification, Asynchronous Event Request with Notice type, Telemetry Log Changed event, Tele

children.title: 8.1.30 Universally Unique Identifiers (UUIDs) for Vendor Specific Information
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements and implementation standards for using Universally Unique Identifiers (UUIDs) to encapsulate vendor-specific data within communication protocols. It addresses how to assign, format, and interpret custom vendor identifiers to ensure interoperability and prevent namespace collisions in proprietary system extensions.

children.title: 8.1.30.1 UUIDs for Vendor Specific Information Introduction
children.description: This section defines the structural requirements and formatting standards for 128-bit Universally Unique Identifiers (UUIDs) used to label vendor-specific data fields. It addresses how developers identify proprietary attributes, clarifies custom service implementation, and explains the bit-masking protocols for ensuring distinct vendor-unique assignments.

children.title: 8.1.30.2 UUIDs for Vendor Specific Information Requirements
children.description: This section defines the use of Universally Unique Identifiers (UUIDs) for disambiguating Vendor Specific information within NVM Express command structures. It clarifies how controllers utilize indexed UUID lists to distinguish between multiple definitions of vendor-specific log pages, features, and data structures.

children.title: 8.1.30.3 UUIDs for Vendor Specific Information Examples
children.description: This section provides implementation examples for utilizing UUIDs to define vendor-specific Log Pages and Features within NVMe-based architectures. It addresses how to structure and identify proprietary device data and diagnostic metadata to ensure interoperability and standard compliance.

children.title: 8.1.30.3.2 Vendor Specific Feature Example
children.description: This section outlines NVMe versioning conventions for firmware updates and UUID implementation strategies for disambiguating overlapping vendor-specific log pages. It addresses how controllers distinguish conflicting log page definitions and the host-side requirements for selecting specific UUID-backed vendor data.

children.title: 8.2 Memory-Based Transport Extended Capabilities (PCIe)
children.description: Section 8.2 details PCIe Memory-Based Transport Extended Capabilities, covering technical specifications for Controller Memory Buffers (CMB), Host Memory Buffers (HMB), Doorbell Strides, and Persistent Memory Regions. It addresses how NVMe subsystems manage direct memory access, software-defined emulation, power-loss resiliency, and virtualization optimizations for high-performance memory transport.

children.title: 8.2.1 Controller Memory Buffer
children.description: This section defines the Controller Memory Buffer (CMB), a PCIe-based read/write memory resource, by detailing its initialization via registers (CAP, CMBMSC, CMBLOC, CMBSZ) and addressing mechanisms. It explains how to enable the CMB for host use and clarifies the distinction between PCIe and controller address ranges for memory-mapped I/O operations.

children.title: 8.2.4 Persistent Memory Region
children.description: This section defines requirements for Controller Memory Buffer (CMB) ordering, doorbell stride calculations for hardware and software emulation, and the implementation of the Host Memory Buffer (HMB) feature. It addresses how memory consistency is maintained during doorbell updates, how to optimize CPU monitoring via cache alignment, and how controllers manage host-assigned memory resources.

children.title: 8.2.5 Power Loss Signaling
children.description: This section details Persistent Memory Region (PMR) write-barrier mechanisms and error-handling requirements for power loss signaling. It specifies how hosts authenticate successful non-volatile writes via the PMRSTS property and utilizes PMRCAP.PMRWBM to ensure data persistence through PCI Express read-based write barriers.

children.title: 8.2.5.1 Power Loss Signaling Processing State Machine
children.description: This section defines the Power Loss Signaling (PLS) state machine, specifying controller behavior during Emergency Power Fail (EPF) and Forced Quiescence (FQ) events. It addresses how non-zero vault and recovery timing fields, namespace readiness (NRDY), and IO intensity (IOI) states dictate recovery operations after power restoration.

children.title: 8.2.5.1.2 PLS Ready State
children.description: Routing description: NVMe Base Spec 2.3 §8.2.5.1

children.title: 8.2.5.1.5 EPF Processing Port Disabled State
children.description: This section defines the EPF (Error-Processing Framework) Processing Port Disabled state, detailing

children.title: 8.2.5.1.8 EPF Complete Port Enabled State
children.description: This section defines the **EPF Complete Port Enabled State** and subsequent transitions within the **NVM Express 2.3** Emergency Power Fail (EPF) logic. It details technical requirements for port state transitions, specifies functional limitations during the **Port Disabled** state, and identifies the triggers—such as **Controller Level Reset** or power cycles—for exiting these terminal power-fail states.

children.title: 8.2.5.2 Forced Quiescence Processing
children.description: This section details the state transition conditions for an NVMe controller during Emergency Power Fail (EPF) processing, specifically addressing Port Disabled and Port Enabled states. It clarifies operational limitations and transition triggers following EPF completion, including conditions for entering the PLS Not Ready state such as power cycling or controller resets.

children.title: 8.2.5.3 Emergency Power Fail Processing
children.description: This section defines the mandatory, sequential recovery procedure for an NVM Express controller entering the **FQ Processing state** during an emergency power failure. It addresses technical requirements for **command queue cessation, power-loss signaling, and background operation suspension** to ensure data integrity during unexpected power loss.

children.title: 8.2.6 Virtualization Enhancements
children.description: This section specifies the technical state transitions, command handling requirements, and recovery procedures for NVM Express controllers during Emergency Power Fail (EPF) events. It addresses operational questions regarding controller state machine logic, command discarding protocols, and the interpretation of EPF recovery metadata (EPFRT/EPFRTS) for system re-initialization.

children.title: 8.2.6.2 VI Resource Definition
children.description: This section details the architectural requirements for Virtualization (VI) and Virtual Queue (VQ) resources, specifically outlining the integration of Primary/Secondary controller relationships, SR-IOV mandates, and resource assignment protocols. It defines the mandatory command support in the Identify Controller structure and identifies controller capabilities necessary to manage Flexible Resources and queue associations within an NVMe subsystem.

children.title: 8.2.6.3 Secondary Controller States and Resource Configuration
children.description: This section defines the management and configuration of Virtual Interrupt (VI) Resources for NVMe secondary controllers, specifically detailing MSI-X requirements, the `MSIXCAP.MXC.TS` read-only state constraints, and the dependency of the Online controller state on assigning a VI resource for interrupt vector 0. It addresses how controllers transition from Offline status via the Virtualization Management command and established resource attribution.

children.title: 8.2.6.4 Single Root I/O Virtualization and Sharing (SR-IOV)
children.description: This section outlines the operational life cycle and resource management for NVMe SR-IOV secondary controllers, including transition conditions for Online/Offline states and PCI-SIG compliance requirements. It specifically addresses how controller state dependencies, resource allocation, and VF Enable settings govern system image access to virtualized I/O resources.

children.title: 8.3 Message-Based Transport Extended Capabilities (Fabrics)
children.description: This section covers NVMe-over-Fabrics (NVMe-oF) transport enhancements, specifically focusing on automated discovery mechanisms, centralized management, cross-controller reset recovery, NVM resource exportation, and secure in-band authentication. It addresses critical questions regarding how fabric endpoints discover services, manage multi-controller resilience, and implement robust security protocols within IP-based storage environments.

children.title: 8.3.1 Automated Discovery of NVMe-oF Discovery Controllers for IP Based Fabrics
children.description: This section details the operational lifecycle and state transitions—specifically the "Online" to "Offline" mechanics—for NVMe-oF secondary controllers managed by a primary controller. It defines the dependencies between controller enablement (CC.EN, CSTS.RDY), resource allocation, and PCI-SIG SR-IOV configuration requirements.

children.title: 8.3.1.1 Discovery of NVMe-oF Discovery Controllers
children.description: This section details the mechanisms for discovering NVMe-oF Discovery Controllers using the NVMe Discovery Service. Key technical topics include query procedures, controller response structures, and transport-level connection requirements used to resolve available NVMe subsystems.

children.title: 8.3.1.1.1 Query
children.description: Section 8.3.1.1.1 defines the mechanisms for automated discovery of NVMe-oF Discovery controllers using DNS-SD via mDNS or DNS servers across local and routed IP fabric topologies. It details specific implementation requirements for hosts, CDCs, and DDCs, including the utilization of mDNS NVMe-oF proxies to bridge communication across Broadcast Domains.

children.title: 8.3.1.1.2 Response
children.description: This section defines the mandatory DNS response structures, specifically detailing the configuration and usage of PTR, SRV, and TXT resource records. It addresses how to implement these DNS query responses to ensure accurate network service discovery and identity verification.

children.title: 8.3.1.1.2.1 DNS PTR record
children.description: This section defines the naming conventions for DNS PTR records used in NVMe-oF service discovery, specifying mandatory formats for mDNS and DNS queries involving protocols, subtypes, and domains. It addresses how NVMe-oF targets advertise services and identifies the required record types—PTR, SRV, TXT, and A/AAAA—necessary to resolve transport addresses for TCP, RoCE, and iWARP fabrics.

children.title: 8.3.1.1.2.3 DNS TXT record
children.description: This section defines the mandatory DNS TXT record structure for NVMe-over-Fabrics Service Discovery, specifically detailing the required "p" (protocol) and optional NQN key/value pairs used for advertising Discovery Controller services. It provides technical specifications for cross-referencing RFC 6763 standards when mapping transport protocols and subsystem identifiers within a DNS-based discovery framework.

children.title: 8.3.1.2 Host Operation
children.description: This section outlines the operational procedures for DNS-SD hosts, focusing on the mechanisms for issuing DNS queries and the logic for processing received resource records. It addresses how hosts discover services by detailing query formulation, response parsing, and service instance identification within a local network.

children.title: 8.3.1.2.1 Host Query
children.description: This section details the construction of DNS SRV and TXT records for host discovery in NVMe over Fabrics networks, specifically defining naming conventions for instance discovery and protocol-specific metadata (p=tcp/roce/iwarp). It provides the technical implementation requirements for mapping NVM subsystems to DNS-based service discovery per RFC 6763.

children.title: 8.3.1.2.2 Host Processing of DNS-SD Records
children.description: This section details NVMe-over-Fabrics host processing of DNS-SD records, focusing on the resolution of `_nvme-disc.<protocol>.local` SRV records for Fabrics Connect command target discovery. It specifies DDC mDNS initialization procedures, including RFC 6762-compliant probing, announcement requirements, and conditional response logic based on whether the DDC is configured for CDC-based push or pull registration.

children.title: 8.3.1.3 DDC Operation
children.description: This section details the operational lifecycle of a Device Discovery Component (DDC), covering mDNS/DNS initialization, query handling, and the parsing of DNS Service Discovery (DNS-SD) records. It addresses how a DDC dynamically discovers services via multicast mechanisms and formats its responses to standard network queries.

children.title: 8.3.1.3.4 DDC Processing of DNS-SD records
children.description: This section details Discovery Data Collection (DDC) mDNS initialization procedures for NVMe/FC and NVMe/TCP discovery services. It defines mandatory link-local unique resource record probes, announcement requirements for RFC 6762 compliance, and conditional mDNS query response logic contingent upon DDC push or pull registration status with a Centralized Discovery Controller (CDC).

children.title: 8.3.1.3.5 DDC response to mDNS queries
children.description: This section defines discovery and addressing procedures for Discovery Data Controllers (DDC) and Centralized Discovery Controllers (CDC) using mDNS protocols. It specifically addresses requirements for mDNS service name formatting, response content, link-local uniqueness probing, and initialization sequences mandated by the NVMe 2.3 specification.

children.title: 8.3.1.4 CDC Operation
children.description: Section 8.3.1.4 covers the operational workflow of the Commissioning Discovery Commissioner (CDC), detailing mDNS and DNS-SD initialization, query mechanisms, and record processing. This technical guide explains how the CDC performs service discovery and manages network responses to ensure cross-device connectivity within the ecosystem.

children.title: 8.3.1.4.5 CDC response to mDNS queries
children.description: This section defines the mandatory mDNS discovery procedures for Centralized Discovery Controllers (CDCs), specifically detailing query responses, mandatory service naming conventions, and link-level initialization requirements per RFC 6762. It addresses how CDCs validate unique resource records and manage mDNS responder enablement during discovery service startup.

children.title: 8.3.2 Centralized Discovery for IP-based Fabrics
children.description: This section details the centralized discovery mechanisms for NVMe/IP fabrics, covering registration protocols, fabric zoning policies, and asynchronous event notifications. It addresses how NVMe Discovery Controllers manage endpoint connectivity, resource visibility, and dynamic state changes within an IP-based storage architecture.

children.title: 8.3.2.1 Overview
children.description: This section details the registration and discovery mechanisms within the system architecture, focusing on service endpoints, protocol handshakes, and resource advertisement. It addresses how nodes locate peers, initiate network presence, and manage metadata exchange.

children.title: 8.3.2.1.1 Registration and Discovery Example
children.description: This section details the NVMe/IP Centralized Discovery Controller (CDC) architecture, covering explicit/implicit registration methods, fabric zoning, and discovery log page filtering. It explains how administrators automate NVM subsystem accessibility for hosts and how devices dynamically register their discovery information within a fabric.

children.title: 8.3.2.2 Discovery Information Registration and De-Registration
children.description: This section outlines the process for registering NVM subsystem fabric interfaces with a Centralized Discovery Controller (CDC) and updating hosts via Discovery Log Page Change asynchronous events. It details how hosts utilize Discovery Log Page entries and the `Get Log Page` command to manage connections to multiple NVM subsystems.

children.title: 8.3.2.2.1 Push Registrations and Push De-Registrations
children.description: This section defines the procedures for establishing and terminating mobile-terminated data delivery via Push Registrations and Push De-Registrations within the 3GPP architecture. It addresses technical requirements for signaling between the User Equipment (UE) and the network to determine how push requests trigger session activation or service de-activation.

children.title: 8.3.2.2.2 Pull Registrations and Pull De-Registrations
children.description: This section defines the mandatory and optional roles of Centralized Discovery Controllers (CDC), Direct Discovery Controllers (DDC), and hosts regarding Discovery Information Management commands. It specifies the operational requirements for push and pull registration and de-registration protocols, detailing which NVMe entities are authorized to use specific TAS (Task) field configurations when managing discovery data.

children.title: 8.3.2.2.2.1 Kickstart Discovery Pull Registration Requests
children.description: This section details the procedures for establishing a kickstart discovery NVMe/TCP connection between a Direct Discovery controller (DDC) and a Centralized Discovery controller (CDC). It defines the technical requirements for TCP connection initiation, the use of the Kickstart Discovery Connection (KDCONN) flag within ICReq PDUs, and the subsequent retrieval of discovery information via KDReq PDUs.

children.title: 8.3.2.3 Fabric Zoning
children.description: This section defines Fiber Channel fabric zoning architecture, covering the management of `ZoneGroups`, `Zones`, and `ZoneAliases` through specific member configurations and operational properties. It answers how to structure and apply access control policies within a storage area network (SAN) and defines the technical procedures for executing zoning operations.

children.title: 8.3.2.3.1 Model
children.description: It outlines the CDC‑based Fabric Zoning model for NVMe‑oF IP fabrics, covering the ZoneDBConfig and ZoneDBActive structures, ZoneGroup enforcement, discovery log page filtering, Change Asynchronous Event notifications, and optional network‑level restrictions. It answers how a Centralized Discovery Controller manages access control, filters discovery log pages, and propagates zoning changes to initiators via Get Log Page commands.

children.title: 8.3.2.3.2 ZoneGroup
children.description: This section defines the structural composition and data fields of a **ZoneGroup** within the **NVM Express (NVMe) fabric zoning model**, specifically detailing **ZGORIG**, **ZGNAME**, and **NUMZ** attributes. It provides the technical specification for how access control rules are organized and represented within the **ZoneDBConfig** and **ZoneDBActive** abstract data structures managed by a **CDC** or **DDC**.

children.title: 8.3.2.3.3 Zone
children.description: This section defines the structural representation, identification, and management of **ZoneGroups** and **Zones** within the NVMe architecture. It outlines key technical parameters, including **ZoneGroup keys**, **generation numbers**, and **Zone Name (ZNAME)** fields, while addressing how access control units are formatted and uniquely identified.

children.title: 8.3.2.3.4 Zone Members
children.description: This section defines the structural specifications and hierarchical member types for NVMe-oF Zoning, including criteria such as NQN, IP address, Protocol, PortID, and Address Family (ADRFAM). It answers how zoning administrators define and categorize access control members within a Fabric to restrict communication between specific host-subsystem entities.

children.title: 8.3.2.3.4.2 {NQN, Role} Zone Member Type (Type 1h)
children.description: Zone member TLV structures, type 1h {NQN, Role} definition, zoning enforcement scope, host‑to‑NVM subsystem communication rules, and applicable NVMe‑Express Base Specification 2.3 sections addressing how zones are identified, paired, and constrained. This section answers which elements are used for zone restriction enforcement, how {NQN, Role} members are represented, and what communication permissions exist between hosts and subsystems.

children.title: 8.3.2.3.4.3 {(NQN, IP, Protocol), Role} Zone Member Type (Type 2h)
children.description: This section defines the Type 2h zone member format for NVMe-over-Fabrics (NVMe-oF) zoning, utilizing a tuple of NQN, IP address, and protocol to assign specific connectivity roles. It specifies the technical configuration requirements for fabric members and addresses how host-to-subsystem access control policies are structured within a zoned storage environment.

children.title: 8.3.2.3.4.4 {(NQN, IP, Protocol, IP Protocol Port), Role} Zone Member Type (Type 3h)
children.description: This section defines the **{(NQN, IP, Protocol, IP Protocol Port), Role} Zone Member Type (Type 3h)** format within the NVMe/FC/TCP fabric, specifying binary encoding requirements for NQN, IP addressing, transport protocols, and roles. It details the structural configuration for identifying NVMe-oF entities to enforce zoning access control and connectivity parameters across fabric interfaces.
